subject: ap-chemistry
title: Specific Heat Capacity & Calorimetry
stimulus: 4. A student performs an experiment to determine the specific heat capacity of a metal. The student places a cube of the metal in boiling water so its temperature will be $100.0^\circ\text{C}$. The student then places the metal cube into a calorimeter that contains water and records the highest temperature of the water. A data table and a diagram of the thermometer at the highest temperature are shown.

parts.prompt: What should the student report as the highest temperature of the water? _____________
parts.scoring_notes: The thermometer diagram shows a reading of 38.5°C. Must be reported to the correct decimal place (tenths).

scoring_points.description: For the correct temperature reading to the correct decimal place: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: The thermometer shows 38.5°C. The meniscus is between the 38 and 39 degree marks, at the 0.5 graduation, so the reading is 38.5°C.

wrong_examples: 38°C or 39°C — thermometer readings should be estimated to one decimal place past the smallest graduation.
38.50°C — too many decimal places for this thermometer scale.
common_traps: Read analog thermometers to one decimal place; the answer must include the tenths digit.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct answer, reported to the correct decimal place: 38.5°C.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct value to the required tenths decimal place. Earns the point.
Missing the tenths digit. Analog thermometers must be read to one decimal place beyond the smallest graduation. No credit.

parts.prompt: A particle-level representation of water molecules in the calorimeter before and after the metal cube was added is shown. The length of the arrows in the Before diagram represents the speed of the water molecules in the system. In the After diagram, draw an arrow for each molecule to indicate how the speed of each of the molecules changes after the metal cube is added.
parts.scoring_notes: After adding the hot metal, the water molecules gain kinetic energy, so they move faster on average. All arrows in the After diagram must be longer than those in the Before diagram.

scoring_points.description: For a correct drawing with longer arrows (higher average speed) in the After diagram: 1 point

required_elements: All arrows in the After diagram are longer on average than in the Before diagram.
alternatives.correct_example: The hot metal transfers thermal energy to the water. The water molecules gain kinetic energy, so their average speed increases. All arrows in the After diagram should be longer than those in the Before diagram.

wrong_examples: Drawing shorter arrows after — the water temperature increased, so molecules move faster.
Drawing arrows of the same length — energy transfer changes particle speed.
common_traps: Temperature is proportional to average kinetic energy. Higher temperature → higher average speed → longer arrows.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for a correct drawing: The 'After' drawing should contain arrows that are longer, on average.

sample_responses.response: [Drawing shows all arrows in the After diagram noticeably longer than in the Before diagram, pointing in various directions.]
sample_responses.commentary: The hot metal transfers thermal energy to the water, raising its temperature. Higher temperature means greater average kinetic energy and faster average molecular speeds, correctly represented by longer arrows. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: [Drawing shows arrows of the same length as in the Before diagram.]
sample_responses.commentary: No change in arrow length implies no change in molecular speed, which contradicts the energy transfer. The water gained thermal energy from the hot metal, so arrows must be longer. No credit.

parts.prompt: Assuming the metal transfers 2940 J of thermal energy to the water, calculate the specific heat of the metal in $\text{J/(g}\cdot^\circ\text{C)}$.
parts.scoring_notes: q_metal = −q_water = −2940 J (metal loses heat). ΔT_metal = 38.5 − 100.0 = −61.5°C. c_metal = q/(m × ΔT); must use consistent values from part (a).

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated specific heat, consistent with part (a): 1 point
wrong_examples: Using ΔT = 38.5 (the final T, not the change) — ΔT must be T_final − T_initial = 38.5 − 100.0.
Reporting a negative specific heat — specific heat is always positive; use |q| and |ΔT|.
common_traps: The metal loses heat: q_metal = −2940 J and ΔT_metal = 38.5 − 100.0 = −61.5°C; the two negatives cancel, giving a positive c.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct calculated value, consistent with part (a): $q = mc\Delta T$; $c_{\text{metal}} = \frac{-2940\text{ J}}{(98.1\text{ g})(38.5^\circ\text{C} - 100.0^\circ\text{C})} = 0.487\text{ J/(g·°C)}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies $q = mc\Delta T$, uses ΔT = T_final − T_initial = 38.5 − 100.0 = −61.5°C, and divides to get a positive specific heat. Earns the point.
Uses only the final temperature (38.5°C) instead of ΔT = T_f − T_i = −61.5°C. This is a common error — ΔT is a change in temperature, not the final temperature. No credit.

parts.prompt: In a second experiment, 2940 J of thermal energy is transferred from 98.1 g of aluminum, which has a specific heat capacity of $0.897 \text{ J/(g}\cdot^\circ\text{C)}$. Explain how the magnitude of the temperature change of the aluminum, $\Delta T_{\text{Al}}$, compares with the magnitude of the temperature change of the metal in the original experiment.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept either a calculation showing ΔT_Al = 33.4°C (vs. ΔT_metal = 61.5°C) or a reasoning-only response: aluminum has a larger specific heat, so for the same q and same mass, ΔT is smaller.

scoring_points.description: For a correct comparison (Al ΔT < original metal ΔT) with valid reasoning: 1 point

required_elements: The temperature change of the aluminum is smaller in magnitude than that of the original metal.
Aluminum has a larger specific heat capacity (0.897 vs. 0.487 J/(g·°C)); for the same mass and same heat transferred, a larger c means a smaller ΔT.
alternatives.correct_example:
$\Delta T_{\text{Al}} = \frac{q}{mc} = \frac{2940\text{ J}}{(98.1\text{ g})(0.897\text{ J/(g·°C)})} = 33.4°\text{C}$ 
This is smaller in magnitude than the original metal's $\Delta T = 61.5°\text{C}$. Aluminum has a larger specific heat capacity, so it requires more energy per gram per degree — meaning the same amount of heat produces a smaller temperature change.

wrong_examples: Saying ΔT_Al is larger — larger specific heat means smaller temperature change for the same q and m.
common_traps: $\Delta T = q/(mc)$: larger c → smaller ΔT for fixed q and m.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for a valid explanation, consistent with part (c). Accept: (1) The value of $\Delta T_{\text{Al}}$ will be smaller because Al has a greater specific heat capacity than the metal in the original experiment. Therefore, the same thermal energy transfer applied to the same mass will result in a smaller change in temperature, according to the equation $q = mc\Delta T$. (2) Calculation showing $\Delta T_{\text{Al}} = 33.4^\circ\text{C}$ vs. $\Delta T_{\text{metal}} = 61.5^\circ\text{C}$.

sample_responses.response: The temperature change of the aluminum will be smaller. Aluminum has a higher specific heat capacity (0.897 J/(g·°C)) than the metal from part (c) (0.487 J/(g·°C)). Since $\Delta T = q/(mc)$, a larger $c$ means a smaller $\Delta T$ for the same $q$ and $m$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly compares specific heat values and uses $q = mc\Delta T$ to explain that larger $c$ produces smaller temperature change. Matches CB's accepted reasoning. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: $\Delta T_{\text{Al}} = \frac{2940}{(98.1)(0.897)} = 33.4^\circ\text{C}$, which is smaller than the original $\Delta T_{\text{metal}} = 61.5^\circ\text{C}$.
sample_responses.commentary: Calculates both temperature changes and compares them directly. CB accepts a quantitative comparison as valid. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The temperature change of the aluminum will be larger because aluminum is more reactive.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrectly predicts a larger temperature change and invokes reactivity, which is irrelevant to calorimetry. Larger specific heat means smaller temperature change for the same heat transfer. No credit.
